author: jsign
content:
Yes, this is a very good point to discuss. I think the tradeoff here is how closer/far we try to try this out in mainnet while minimizing the risk as much as possible whiel getting valuable data. Ofc, the way to minimize risk as much as possible is to leave out validators from opt-in, but it also means we might learn less on exactly the concern you describe (which we also agree and is in fact related to the unknowns we want to discover).

For example, [hardware requirements](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7870) for validators and full-nodes are different, so this is also important context that might shift a the value/conclusions we can extract from this running on mainnet. It is also true that those requirements are strictly framed for current re-execution validation, so it is important that we correctly communicate which validators would be recommended to turn on this experimental feature.

I don’t think there is a right/wrong take on this but mostly tradeoffs, so it is quite helpful to get feedback from people about this.

author: jsign
content: I think how to communicate properly which validators are recommended to enable this will be the main point to be careful and do properly. Ideally we want a small percentage of nodes with probably higher resources than the recommended in the EIP to start experimenting, and not everyone turning this on blindly.

author: nero_eth
content:
I see! This makes sense and clarifies it for me, thanks!
I definitely see your points (e.g. validator hw requirments are better is a good point). So, it's a trade-off between "how realistic we want it for testing vs lowest possible impact on the live network".
Assuming we have a few validators with higher hw requirments, those might be the best candidates to opt-in first, but of course, our "best" nodes opting in could mean that worse nodes receive blocks later too (simply because their best peers are now also busy propagating proofs causing them to turn from "good" to "average" peers).
